title: The Northern Version of Cato's Distichs
summary: CME00083 is the checked-in copy of the University of Michigan encoding of Max Förster's complete 1906 article 'Eine nordenglische Cato-Version.' Förster prints two divergent manuscript versions on alternating pages: Sidney Sussex College MS 63 on even pages 4–54 and Bodleian MS. Rawl. G. 59 on odd pages 5–55, preceded by his German introduction on pages 1–3. Aage Brusendorff identified a third witness in Royal Danish Library Thott 306 folio, which he described as earlier and textually better, and printed selected passages plus one manuscript photograph in 1929. DIMEV 6324 now records all three witnesses. Complete scans of both historical print containers and the complete DIMEV v1.0 release are cached locally. No complete public facsimile was found for any of the three physical manuscripts on 2026-07-12. The only public manuscript image verified is Brusendorff's monochrome plate of the upper half of Thott fol. A2r; it is explicitly partial. Whole-object reproduction routes therefore ask for every present manuscript surface and separately map the work loci within the full codices.

entities.label: General Corpus CME00083 XML artifact
entities.description: The exact XML file checked into the pinned CME submodule.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME electronic text CME00083
entities.description: A manually keyed, SGML/TEI-derived encoding of Förster's article, including the German introduction, both manuscript streams, notes, page breaks, folio milestones, and editorial italics.

entities.label: Anonymous Northern Middle English version of Cato's Distichs
entities.description: The abstract Middle English verse translation and adaptation represented by three divergent witnesses. It is not any one codex, witness spelling, scholarly edition, or reconstructed critical text.

entities.label: Disticha Catonis
entities.description: The late-antique Latin moral and educational work translated directly in the Northern Middle English version. The English translator's exact Latin exemplar is unidentified; Förster's use of Baehrens for reference numbering does not make that modern edition a medieval source.

entities.label: Max Förster, Eine nordenglische Cato-Version (1906)
entities.description: The immediate source edition for CME00083. It supplies a German study and complete separate transcriptions of the Sidney and Rawlinson versions rather than a single eclectic critical text.

bibliographic.citation: Max Förster, 'Eine nordenglische Cato-Version,' Englische Studien 36 (1906): 1–55.
bibliographic.title: Eine nordenglische Cato-Version

entities.label: Johnson Reprint Corporation photographic reprint of Englische Studien 36
entities.description: The complete 1965 photographic reprint volume imaged by the Internet Archive scan.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Englische Studien 36
Sidney Sussex College MS 63
entities.description: A paper Confessio Amantis codex with the Northern Cato translation added at ff. 202vb–208rb. This witness is one representation of the work, not its source manuscript or a chapter of a composite work.

holding.notes: No repository-owned exact item record was identified. The separately modeled Pearsall-Mooney scholarly catalog chapter and Sidney Special Collections page provide description and access routes without masquerading as a holding catalog URL.

physical_description.support: Paper, with a separately kept parchment replacement or flyleaf at the beginning
physical_description.extent: James reports 210 leaves; modern foliation extends through at least f. 211v. The Cato occupies ff. 202vb–208rb.
physical_description.format: Double columns, historically described as 43 lines per column
notes: Pearsall and Mooney describe one early-to-mid-fifteenth-century scribe writing both Confessio Amantis and Cato.
The modern locus ff. 202vb–208rb refines James's older 202b–208a description and Förster's 202b–208a notation.
DIMEV associates the manuscript language with eLALME 1.64, Ely; this witness-level scribal localization does not displace Förster's work-level Northern rhyme analysis.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Rawl. G. 59
entities.description: A fifteenth-century English paper codex containing the Latin Disticha Catonis with Middle English translation. Förster prints a strongly revised witness version.

date_statements.label: production, Förster following Madan
date_statements.value: third quarter of the fifteenth century

physical_description.support: Paper, with a pair of parchment flyleaves at each end
physical_description.extent: iii later endleaves + 16 leaves. The current catalogue broadly infers the item at ff. 1r–16v; DIMEV gives ff. 1–13; Förster and CME milestones delimit the printed Middle English stream at ff. 1r–11v.
notes: The differing work loci describe different cataloging and edition boundaries and require repository confirmation before a requested facsimile is mapped.
The probable Dartford Priory association rests on 'Suster Emme Wynter' at f. 13r.

entities.label: Royal Danish Library Thott 306 folio
entities.description: The third and textually best witness identified by Brusendorff. The complete 14-leaf parchment codex contains only the Latin Disticha and its Middle English verse rendering, with each Latin distich followed by the English paraphrase.

date_statements.label: production, current Royal Danish Library catalogue

physical_description.extent: 14 leaves in two quires of eight and six leaves; the work occupies the complete manuscript, ff. 1–14.
physical_description.format: One bound volume in a bookbinder's case; Latin distich followed by Middle English paraphrase
notes: Brusendorff dates the hand to the close of the fourteenth century; the current Royal Danish Library record gives only the fifteenth century. Both attributed datings are retained.
The current record reports reading-room delivery and no electronic service.

entities.label: Aage Brusendorff, He Knew Nat Catoun for His Wit Was Rude (1929)
entities.description: A study of Cato traditions that identifies and describes Thott 306, prints selected Latin and Middle English passages from it, compares them with Förster's two witnesses, and reproduces one partial manuscript photograph.

bibliographic.citation: Aage Brusendorff, 'He Knew Nat Catoun for His Wit Was Rude,' in Studies in English Philology: A Miscellany in Honor of Frederick Klaeber, ed. Kemp Malone and Martin B. Ruud (Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 1929), 320–339.
bibliographic.title: He Knew Nat Catoun for His Wit Was Rude
bibliographic.container_title: Studies in English Philology: A Miscellany in Honor of Frederick Klaeber

entities.description: A single monochrome printed plate showing only the upper half of one Thott leaf. It is not a facsimile of the complete manuscript and does not replace a whole-object request.

access.access_method: Ask the Archivist whether a complete color digital reproduction of the current physical MS 63 can be supplied. Specify covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, all endleaves and blanks, every recto and verso, the separately sleeved parchment leaf, cancelled-leaf stubs and detached matter where present. Separately identify Cato at ff. 202vb–208rb and ask the repository to confirm the current foliation and complete-object inventory.

local_copies.label: Royal Danish Library digitisation services and prices
Royal Danish Library copyright and use guidance

notes: Current foreign-user prices list PDF at DKK 10 per ordinary A4/A3 page or DKK 15 per difficult/valuable page, with a DKK 250 minimum and DKK 5,000 per-volume maximum for the latter category; JPEG is DKK 30 per file with a DKK 600 minimum; high-resolution hardcover imaging is DKK 425 per file including VAT for originals up to A1. The library determines feasibility and quotes the order.

The library reserves the right to make a purchased digitisation freely available later. Ask explicitly whether the delivered files may be redistributed in Git and used in commercial or print-on-demand publication.
If complete coverage cannot be supplied, request a written list of omitted surfaces and record the delivery as partial.

access.access_method: Order the available manuscript through the library system for consultation in the Black Diamond research reading room under current special regulations.

rights.basis: The U-M CME collection states that its public-domain source editions and collection materials are public domain in the United States and applies No Copyright–United States.

rights.component: Medieval Middle English and Latin linguistic and literary content
rights.basis: The work and all three manuscript witnesses are medieval and centuries outside copyright term. This does not grant rights in modern photographs, catalogs, or editions.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1906 and is in the public domain in the United States.

rights.basis: The article was published in 1929 and entered the United States public domain by 2026.

notes: Ask for written clarification of Git redistribution, public display, and commercial or print-on-demand reuse. The library may later make purchased digitisation available to other users, but that statement alone is not a license for the requester to redistribute the files.

rights.basis: The modern chapter remains within ordinary copyright term and Cambridge Core provides it under subscription or purchase terms, not an open-content license.

editorial_practices.representation: Sidney on even pages 4–54 and Rawlinson on odd pages 5–55, with corresponding stanza and line numbering
editorial_practices.description: Förster prints both witnesses separately in an alternating-page comparison and discusses Sidney as preserving more original wording and Rawlinson as strongly revised. The result is not one composite medieval text.

editorial_practices.representation: two consecutive DIV2 version streams, Sidney first and Rawlinson second
editorial_practices.description: The XML preserves the witness texts but serializes Förster's page-level alternation. The 1,265 L elements are therefore the sum of two representations of one work, not the line count of a single authored poem.

editorial_practices.source_feature: manuscript abbreviations and editorially supplied letters
editorial_practices.representation: italic type in the printed transcriptions; HI REND='italic' in CME00083
editorial_practices.description: Many internal italics function as expanded or supplied letters, but Förster's three-page introduction does not provide a complete global statement of the convention. The italics are evidence of the edition's intervention and must not be treated as facsimile representations of the original abbreviation marks without checking manuscript images.
U-M structurally preserves the printed italics. It does not reverse Förster's editorial expansions into the scribes' original abbreviation signs.

editorial_practices.source_feature: complete Thott text on fourteen leaves
editorial_practices.representation: praefationes and passages selected for important divergences, plus one half-leaf plate
editorial_practices.description: Brusendorff explicitly limits his transcription to selected passages. His article and plate document the third witness but are not a complete textual or photographic surrogate.
editorial_practices.scope: Printed pp. 323–338 and the unnumbered plate after p. 326.

notes: Förster's footnote contains typographic or OCR-prone catalog references; current shelfmarks and item numbers are independently controlled here.

open_questions.question: Should Thott 306 be dated to the close of the fourteenth century, as Brusendorff argued, or only broadly to the fifteenth century, as the current Royal Danish Library record states?
next_steps: Request the current specialist manuscript description or dating rationale from Royal Danish Library.
Retain both attributed datings until a current paleographical assessment resolves the difference.

open_questions.question: How do Förster's ff. 1r–11v transcription boundary, DIMEV's ff. 1–13 locus, and the current Bodleian record's inferred ff. 1r–16v item boundary correspond in the physical codex?
next_steps: Ask the Bodleian to confirm current foliation and identify the last Middle English line of DIMEV 6324.
Map all three boundary claims against a complete reproduction when obtained.

open_questions.question: What stemmatic relationship does current scholarship support among Thott, Sidney, and Rawlinson?
next_steps: Consult a modern critical study or edition of DIMEV 6324.
Do not convert Förster's or Brusendorff's qualitative judgments into direct-copy relations among the surviving codices without stemmatic evidence.

open_questions.question: Can the three repositories supply complete color facsimiles, and under what Git, public-viewer, scholarly-publication, and commercial print-on-demand terms?
next_steps: Request whole-object quotes and inventories from all three repositories.
Record each delivered file's exact terms and coverage before public redistribution.
Prefer the complete codex even when the work occupies only a portion, and retain exact work-to-folio mappings.

review_notes: The third Thott witness is absent from Förster and therefore from CME00083; it is included as work-lineage evidence, not as an encoded source.
Whole-manuscript facsimile coverage is the target for future downloads. The one verified public manuscript plate remains marked partial and is never substituted for the complete Thott codex.
The local source cache is gitignored. Four shared public support files are true hard links: DIMEV v1.0 and the three Bodleian forms or terms documents.
